content:
# Leviticus Chapter 10 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)

**Translator:** SOPHIA (Consensus Arbiter) | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic) | **Base Text:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 63r–63v | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 94–95

---

*[Structural note: Leviticus 10 is the immediate and devastating sequel to the theophanic climax of chapter 9. The same divine fire that consumed the offerings in acceptance (9:24) now consumes two of the newly installed priests in judgment (10:2). The verbal echo is exact and intended: vattetse esh mi-lifney YHWH vattokal ("fire came out from before the LORD and consumed") appears in both 9:24 and 10:2, with only the object changing — the offerings in chapter 9, the offerers in chapter 10. The chapter divides into three panels: (1) vv. 1–7: the death of Nadab and Abihu by unauthorized fire, Moses' theological interpretation, the removal of the bodies, and the prohibition on mourning for Aaron's household; (2) vv. 8–11: the LORD's direct address to Aaron prohibiting wine and strong drink during priestly service, with rationale; (3) vv. 12–20: Moses' instructions on eating the holy portions and the crisis of the uneaten sin-offering goat, resolved by Aaron's defense. MT and English versification are identical throughout.]*

---

**1** Now Aaron's sons Nadab and Abihu each took his fire-pan, put fire in it, laid incense on it, and offered unauthorized fire before the LORD — fire he had not commanded them. **2** And fire came out from before the LORD and consumed them, and they died before the LORD.

**3** Then Moses said to Aaron, "This is what the LORD spoke, saying: 'Among those who draw near to me I will show myself holy, and before all the people I will be glorified.'" And Aaron was silent.

---

**4** And Moses called Mishael and Elzaphan, the sons of Uzziel — Aaron's uncle — and said to them, "Come near and carry your brothers away from the front of the sanctuary to a place outside the camp." **5** So they came near and carried them in their tunics outside the camp, as Moses had said.

---

**6** And Moses said to Aaron and to Eleazar and Ithamar his sons, "Do not let your hair hang loose, and do not tear your garments — lest you die, and wrath come upon all the congregation. But your brothers, the whole house of Israel, may weep over the burning that the LORD has kindled. **7** And you shall not go out from the entrance of the tent of meeting, lest you die, for the anointing oil of the LORD is upon you." And they did according to the word of Moses.

---

**8** And the LORD spoke to Aaron, saying:

**9** "Wine and strong drink you shall not drink — you or your sons with you — when you enter the tent of meeting, lest you die. It is a statute forever throughout your generations: **10** to distinguish between the holy and the common, and between the unclean and the clean, **11** and to teach the sons of Israel all the statutes that the LORD has spoken to them through Moses."

---

**12** And Moses spoke to Aaron and to Eleazar and Ithamar, his remaining sons: "Take the grain offering that remains from the LORD's fire offerings and eat it unleavened beside the altar, for it is most holy. **13** You shall eat it in a holy place, for it is your due and your sons' due from the LORD's fire offerings — for so I have been commanded. **14** But the breast of the wave offering and the thigh of the contribution you shall eat in a clean place — you and your sons and your daughters with you — for they have been given as your due and your sons' due from the sacrifices of the peace offerings of the sons of Israel.

**15** "The thigh of the contribution and the breast of the wave offering they shall bring with the fire offerings of the fat, to wave as a wave offering before the LORD; and it shall be for you and for your sons with you as a due forever, as the LORD has commanded."

---

**16** Now Moses diligently sought the goat of the sin offering — and behold, it had been burned up. And he was angry with Eleazar and Ithamar, the remaining sons of Aaron, saying: **17** "Why did you not eat the sin offering in the holy place? For it is most holy, and he gave it to you to bear the iniquity of the congregation, to make atonement for them before the LORD. **18** Behold — its blood was not brought into the inner sanctuary. You certainly should have eaten it in the holy place, as I commanded."

**19** And Aaron spoke to Moses: "Behold — today they offered their sin offering and their burnt offering before the LORD, and yet such things as these have happened to me. If I had eaten the sin offering today, would it have been pleasing in the sight of the LORD?"

**20** And Moses heard, and it was pleasing in his sight.

---

## Council Notes

### On the Chapter

Leviticus 10 is the most theologically charged chapter in the entire sacrificial legislation of Leviticus, and among the most difficult anywhere in the Pentateuch. Three large observations shape SOPHIA's approach.

**First: the inversion of chapter 9's fire.** The verbal echo between 9:24 and 10:2 is the structural and theological key to the whole chapter. All three agents recognize it; all three render it with the same verb "consumed." The consensus preserves this echo with deliberate care — the fire of divine acceptance and the fire of divine judgment use the same language because they are the same fire. The consensus text does not explain this in the verse itself; the structural note above alerts the careful reader.

**Second: Aaron's silence.** The two Hebrew words va-yiddom Aharon stand as one of the most charged moments in all of Scripture. EMET and LOGOS render it "Aaron was silent." PNEUMA renders it identically but provides the most illuminating commentary of the three agents on its depth. The consensus adopts "Aaron was silent" — plain, unadorned, without emotional interpretation — following the MT's restraint against the LXX's κατενύχθη ("was pierced with compunction"). The silence must be allowed to do its own work.

**Third: the resolution in verses 19–20.** Aaron's defense and Moses' acceptance constitute the only moment in Leviticus where Moses explicitly yields to another's reasoning. The legal-pastoral tension — written rule (eat the sin offering) versus extraordinary circumstances (the day his sons were consumed by divine fire) — is resolved not by a new divine command but by a human exchange. SOPHIA's consensus renders this exchange with the restraint and gravity it deserves. PNEUMA's "Moses heard this and approved" is too breezy a closing; the consensus retains the full eye-idiom construction.

### Key Term Rulings

| Term | Hebrew | Consensus Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| esh zarah | אֵשׁ זָרָה | unauthorized fire | Established task-brief standard; preferred over "strange fire" (KJV) and "alien fire"; captures the legal-cultic sense of zarah in the priestly context |
| chattat | חַטָּאת | sin offering | Series standard |
| olah | עֹלָה | burnt offering | Series standard |
| machtah | מַחְתָּה | fire-pan | EMET's rendering; "censer" (common alternative) is less precise — the machtah is a coal-carrying vessel, not a swinging thurible |
| bi-qerovai eqqadesh | בִּקְרֹבַי אֶקָּדֵשׁ | Among those who draw near to me I will show myself holy | "Show myself holy" preferred over "be sanctified" (LOGOS) — the niphal of qadash here is YHWH's self-manifestation, not something done to him; "draw near to me" preferred over "who are near me" (EMET/LOGOS) to preserve the dynamic/active sense of the qrv root |
| va-yiddom Aharon | וַיִּדֹּם אַהֲרֹן | Aaron was silent | MT restraint followed; LXX κατενύχθη not adopted |
| para' (hair) | פָּרַע | let your hair hang loose | LOGOS standard adopted; "uncover your heads" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but less precise |
| ha-qodesh / ha-chol | הַקֹּדֶשׁ / הַחֹל | the holy / the common | Series standard; "profane" rejected (βέβηλος / LXX intensification); chol is morally neutral in the MT |
| ha-tame' / ha-tahor | הַטָּמֵא / הַטָּהוֹר | the unclean / the clean | Series standard |
| chuqqat olam | חֻקַּת עוֹלָם | a statute forever | "Permanent statute" (PNEUMA) acceptable but "statute forever" is more literal and matches series standard |
| bney yisrael | בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל | sons of Israel | Series standard; "people of Israel" (LOGOS) and "Israelites" (PNEUMA) rejected |
| qodesh qodashim | קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים | most holy | Series standard |
| la-set et-avon ha-edah | לָשֵׂאת אֶת-עֲוֹן הָעֵדָה | to bear the iniquity of the congregation | "Bear the iniquity" (EMET/LOGOS) over "remove the guilt" (PNEUMA) — nasa' is a bearing/carrying verb; "remove" (PNEUMA/LXX ἀφαιρέω) interprets the result rather than the action |
| ha-notarim | הַנּוֹתָרִים | remaining sons (or: the remaining ones) | "Remaining" preferred over "surviving" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for vv. 12 and 16; "surviving" is not wrong but carries an implication of escape from danger that the Hebrew noun (from yatar, "to remain") does not necessarily bear in v. 12; for v. 16, where the context of the deaths is clear, both are acceptable — consensus uses "remaining" throughout for consistency |
| vayyitav be-eynav | וַיִּיטַב בְּעֵינָיו | it was pleasing in his sight | Full idiom retained; "he approved" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is too compressed for the closing verse of the chapter |

### Verse-by-Verse Decisions

**vv. 1–2 — Paragraph break.** EMET treats vv. 1–2 as two separate numbered paragraphs with a full stop between. LOGOS and PNEUMA run them together in a single paragraph. Consensus: vv. 1–2 are run together as LOGOS/PNEUMA — the wayyiqtol chain (each took... put fire... laid incense... offered... fire came out... consumed them... they died) forms a single rapid narrative unit; a paragraph break between them interrupts the verbal momentum.

**v. 1 — "fire-pan" vs. "censer."** EMET: "fire-pan." LOGOS and PNEUMA: "censer." Consensus: "fire-pan" (EMET). The machtah (מַחְתָּה) is a flat-bottomed metal vessel for transporting live coals — it is not a swinging perfume-burner ("censer"). The precision matters because the precise identity of the vessel underlies the nature of the offense (they took the coal-carrier associated with incense transport and used it without authorization).

**v. 1 — "laid" vs. "placed" vs. "put."** EMET: "placed." LOGOS: "laid." PNEUMA: "laid." Consensus: "laid" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — vayasimu is the standard verb of placement; "laid" is the natural English idiom for placing incense on top of burning coals.

**v. 1 — "offered unauthorized fire" vs. "brought near unauthorized fire."** EMET: "brought near... esh zarah." LOGOS: "offered unauthorized fire." PNEUMA: "offered unauthorized fire." Consensus: "offered unauthorized fire" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — vayaqrivu (hiphil of qrv) in a cultic context is "offered/presented" to the LORD; "brought near" (EMET) is the more literal rendering of the hiphil but is slightly awkward in English; "offered" is the standard cultic English idiom.

**v. 1 — em-dash construction.** EMET runs a long sentence with brackets. LOGOS uses a simple relative clause. PNEUMA uses a dash. Consensus: dash before "fire he had not commanded them," following PNEUMA's clean construction — the dash creates a slight pause that marks the devastating theological qualifier. Full sentence: "...and offered unauthorized fire before the LORD — fire he had not commanded them."

**v. 2 — "And fire came out" vs. "Fire came out."** LOGOS omits the waw-consecutive opening. EMET: "And fire came out." PNEUMA: "Fire came out." Consensus: "And fire came out" (EMET/LOGOS restored) — the waw-consecutive (vattetse) is a continuing narrative connector; the "And" preserves the immediate narrative consequence and, critically, creates the verbal parallel with 9:24 that English readers need to feel.

**v. 3 — "Then Moses said" vs. "And Moses said" vs. "Moses said."** EMET: "And Moshe said." LOGOS: "Then Moses said." PNEUMA: "Moses said to Aaron." Consensus: "Then Moses said to Aaron" (LOGOS) — the transitional "Then" is appropriate at the shift from narrative description (vv. 1–2) to Moses' theological interpretation (v. 3); "And Moses said" is too flat; the bare "Moses said" (PNEUMA without the transition) reads as continuation rather than pivot.

**v. 3 — "what the LORD spoke saying" vs. "what the LORD has spoken."** EMET: "what the LORD spoke... saying." LOGOS: "what the LORD has spoken." PNEUMA: "what the LORD meant when he said." Consensus: "what the LORD spoke, saying" (EMET adapted) — the phrase hu' asher dibber YHWH lemor is a citation formula; "this is what the LORD spoke, saying" is the most precise rendering, preserving the quotative lemor; "what the LORD has spoken" (LOGOS) is acceptable; "what the LORD meant when he said" (PNEUMA) is interpretively expansive and unjustified.

**v. 3 — "Among those who draw near to me I will show myself holy."** EMET: "Among those near me I will show myself holy." LOGOS: "Among those who are near me I will be sanctified." PNEUMA: "Among those who come near me I will show myself holy." Consensus: "Among those who draw near to me I will show myself holy" — combining PNEUMA's active verb sense ("draw near" rather than the static "are near") with EMET's rendering of eqqadesh as "show myself holy" rather than LOGOS's "be sanctified." The niphal of qadash here is YHWH's self-manifestation in judgment; "show myself holy" best captures this. "Draw near to me" preserves the dynamic priestly approach (qrv root) better than "near me" or "who are near me."

**v. 3 — "before all the people" vs. "before all the assembly."** EMET and LOGOS: "before all the people." PNEUMA: "before all the people." All three agree. Consensus: "before all the people" — kol-ha-am; the LXX's συναγωγή ("assembly") for ha-am here is a slight shift; "people" is the more direct rendering and matches the Hebrew.

**v. 3 — "And Aaron was silent."** All three agents agree. Consensus: "And Aaron was silent." No elaboration in the text.

**v. 4 — "Come near" vs. "Draw near" vs. "Come forward."** EMET: "Draw near." LOGOS: "Come near." PNEUMA: "Come forward." Consensus: "Come near" (LOGOS) — qirvu is the imperative of qrv; "come near" is the natural English command form; "draw near" (EMET) sounds overly formal as a spoken command; "come forward" (PNEUMA) is idiomatic but changes the spatial directionality from proximity to movement.

**v. 4 — "carry your brothers" vs. "carry your relatives."** EMET and LOGOS: "carry your brothers." PNEUMA: "carry your relatives." Consensus: "carry your brothers" (EMET/LOGOS) — the Hebrew is acheykhem ("your brothers"); Mishael and Elzaphan are first cousins of Nadab and Abihu, not brothers, but the Hebrew 'ach can designate kinship more broadly; "brothers" is the more literal rendering; "relatives" (PNEUMA) is accurate but interpretively loose.

**v. 4 — "the front of the sanctuary" vs. "the holy place."** EMET: "before the holy place." LOGOS: "the front of the sanctuary." PNEUMA: "the front of the sanctuary." Consensus: "the front of the sanctuary" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — mi-lifney ha-qodesh means "from before the holy place/sanctuary"; "the front of the sanctuary" renders the locative sense clearly; "before the holy place" (EMET) is accurate but slightly stilted.

**v. 4 — "uncle of Aaron" vs. "Aaron's uncle."** EMET: "the uncle of Aharon." LOGOS: "the uncle of Aaron." PNEUMA: "Aaron's uncle." Consensus: "Aaron's uncle" (PNEUMA) — dod Aharon; the possessive is the more natural English construction.

**v. 5 — "as Moses had said" vs. "as Moses had spoken" vs. "as Moses had commanded."** EMET: "as Moshe had spoken." LOGOS: "as Moses had said." PNEUMA: "as Moses had said." Consensus: "as Moses had said" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — ka-asher dibber Moshe; "said" is the idiomatic English equivalent; "spoken" (EMET) is accurate but slightly formal; the underlying Hebrew is the same.

**v. 6 — Addressees.** EMET: "to Aharon and to Eleazar and to Itamar his sons." LOGOS: "to Aaron and to Eleazar and Ithamar his sons." PNEUMA: "to Aaron and his sons Eleazar and Ithamar." Consensus: "to Aaron and to Eleazar and Ithamar his sons" (LOGOS) — the MT has three separate address objects (el-Aharon ve-el-Eleazar ve-el-Itamar banav); the two-"to" rendering (LOGOS) matches the MT's rhythm while avoiding the cumbersome three-"and-to" of EMET; PNEUMA's restructuring ("his sons Eleazar and Ithamar") is idiomatic but loses the syntactic individuation of each address.

**v. 6 — "Do not let your hair hang loose" vs. "Do not uncover your heads."** EMET: "Your heads you shall not uncover." LOGOS: "Do not let your hair hang loose." PNEUMA: "Do not uncover your heads." Consensus: "Do not let your hair hang loose" (LOGOS) — rosheykhem lo' tifrau; para' refers specifically to the loosening/disheveling of hair as a mourning gesture (cf. Lev 13:45; Num 5:18); "let your hair hang loose" captures the specific gesture; "uncover your heads" (EMET/PNEUMA) is the LXX's reading (ἀποκαλύπτω) and slightly shifts the gesture from hair-dishevelment to head-uncovering; the MT's para' is the more specific.

**v. 6 — "lest you die, and wrath come upon all the congregation."** EMET: "lest you die and upon all the congregation he be wrathful." LOGOS: "lest you die, and wrath come upon all the congregation." PNEUMA: "or you will die, and anger will fall on the whole community." Consensus: "lest you die, and wrath come upon all the congregation" (LOGOS) — the conditional pen ("lest") is the established series rendering; "or you will die" (PNEUMA) is natural but loses the warning-conditional force; "upon all the congregation" (ha-edah) is the series standard term; "whole community" (PNEUMA) loses ha-edah's specificity.

**v. 6 — "the burning that the LORD has kindled" vs. "the burning that the LORD has caused."** EMET: "the burning which the LORD has set ablaze." LOGOS: "the burning that the LORD has kindled." PNEUMA: "the burning that the LORD has caused." Consensus: "the burning that the LORD has kindled" (LOGOS) — ha-sreifah asher saraf YHWH; "kindled" renders saraf with natural English idiom while preserving the fire imagery; "set ablaze" (EMET) is slightly more vivid but also more dramatic; "caused" (PNEUMA) is too neutral and loses the fire imagery of saraf entirely.

**v. 7 — "according to the word of Moses" vs. "as Moses had said" vs. "as Moses said."** EMET: "according to the word of Moses." LOGOS: "according to the word of Moses." PNEUMA: "as Moses said." Consensus: "according to the word of Moses" (EMET/LOGOS) — ki-devar Moshe; "according to the word of Moses" is the more precise rendering of the phrase; "as Moses said" (PNEUMA) loses the formal authority marker "the word of" (devar).

**vv. 8–9 — Section break and address.** EMET treats vv. 8 and 9 as separate verse-paragraph units. LOGOS and PNEUMA merge them. Consensus: verse 8 ("And the LORD spoke to Aaron, saying:") stands as a separate address-marker, and verse 9 begins the speech — following the LOGOS/EMET paragraph logic; the unique addressee (Aaron alone, not Moses then Aaron) is theologically significant and worth the typographical clarity of the break.

**v. 9 — "Wine and strong drink you shall not drink" vs. "Drink no wine or strong drink" vs. "You and your sons are not to drink wine or strong drink."** EMET: "Wine... and strong drink... you shall not drink." LOGOS: "Drink no wine or strong drink." PNEUMA: "You and your sons are not to drink wine or strong drink." Consensus: "Wine and strong drink you shall not drink" (EMET order adapted) — the MT places yayin ve-shekhar at the head of the clause (emphatic topicalization); "Wine and strong drink you shall not drink" preserves this emphasis; LOGOS's "Drink no wine or strong drink" normalizes the English word order and loses the emphasis; PNEUMA's reordering is smooth but also loses it.

**v. 9 — "when you enter" vs. "when you go into."** EMET: "when you enter." LOGOS: "when you go into." PNEUMA: "when you enter." Consensus: "when you enter" (EMET/PNEUMA) — bevaakhem el-ohel mo'ed; "enter" is the more natural English verb for coming into an enclosed sacred space; "go into" (LOGOS) is slightly informal.

**v. 9 — "It is a statute forever" vs. "It shall be a statute forever."** EMET: "it is a statute forever." LOGOS: "It shall be a statute forever." PNEUMA: "This is a permanent statute." Consensus: "It is a statute forever" (EMET) — chuqqat olam hi'; the verbless Hebrew clause renders most naturally as the present-declarative "It is a statute forever"; the future "shall be" (LOGOS) imports a prospective nuance not in the MT; "permanent statute" (PNEUMA) replaces olam ("forever") with "permanent," which is weaker.

**v. 10 — Sentence structure.** All three agents agree on "to distinguish between the holy and the common, and between the unclean and the clean." Consensus: retained as is, with minimal variation — "You are to distinguish" (LOGOS) is adopted as the governing verb to link vv. 9–11 as a purpose-clause unit subordinate to the entry into the tent of meeting.

**v. 11 — "sons of Israel" vs. "people of Israel" vs. "Israelites."** LOGOS: "people of Israel." PNEUMA: "Israelites." EMET: "sons of Yisra'el." Consensus: "sons of Israel" — bney Yisrael is the series standard; the change from "people of Israel" (LOGOS) is consistent with the series ruling established from Genesis.

**v. 11 — "through Moses" vs. "by Moses" vs. "given them through Moses."** EMET: "through Moshe." LOGOS: "by Moses." PNEUMA: "through Moses." Consensus: "through Moses" (EMET/PNEUMA) — be-yad Moshe is "by the hand of Moses"; "through Moses" captures both the mediation and the instrumentality of the phrase; "by Moses" (LOGOS) is slightly more ambiguous (could imply direct authorship rather than mediation).

**v. 12 — "his remaining sons" vs. "the ones remaining" vs. "his surviving sons."** EMET: "the ones remaining." LOGOS: "his remaining sons." PNEUMA: "his remaining sons Eleazar and Ithamar." Consensus: "his remaining sons" (LOGOS) — ha-notarim modifying banav; "remaining" is adopted over "surviving" for the reasons stated in the Key Term Rulings table.

**v. 12 — "Take the grain offering" vs. "take the grain offering left over."** EMET: "Take the grain offering that remains." LOGOS: "Take the grain offering that remains." PNEUMA: "Take the grain offering left over." All three agree on the substance. Consensus: "Take the grain offering that remains" (EMET/LOGOS) — the ha-noteret participial clause modifies the minchah; the relative-clause rendering ("that remains") is slightly more precise than the appositive ("left over").

**v. 13 — "for it is your due and your sons' due" vs. "because it is your due and your sons' due."** All three agents agree on the substance. Consensus: "for it is your due and your sons' due" — ki chaqekha ve-chaq-banekha; "for" (causal) is the appropriate English connector; "because" (PNEUMA alternative) is a synonym and acceptable but "for" is the series standard for ki in legal rationale clauses.

**v. 13 — "for so I have been commanded" vs. "that is what I have been commanded."** EMET: "for thus I have been commanded." LOGOS: "for so I am commanded." PNEUMA: "that is what I have been commanded." Consensus: "for so I have been commanded" (EMET adapted) — ki ken tsuvveyti; the passive past "have been commanded" (EMET) is more accurate than "am commanded" (LOGOS present tense); PNEUMA's "that is what" is idiomatic but slightly wordy.

**v. 14 — "the breast of the wave offering and the thigh of the contribution."** All three agents agree on this phrase. Consensus: retained — "the breast of the wave offering and the thigh of the contribution" — the established Levitical technical terms.

**v. 14 — "the sons of Israel" vs. "the people of Israel" vs. "the Israelites."** LOGOS: "the people of Israel." PNEUMA: "the Israelites." EMET: "the sons of Yisra'el." Consensus: "the sons of Israel" — series standard throughout.

**v. 15 — "as a due forever" vs. "as your and your children's permanent portion."** EMET and LOGOS: "as a due forever." PNEUMA: "as your and your children's permanent portion." Consensus: "as a due forever" (EMET/LOGOS) — le-choq olam; "forever" is the rendering of olam in the sacrificial context, and "due" is the established rendering of choq; PNEUMA's "permanent portion" substitutes both key terms.

**v. 16 — "Moses diligently sought" vs. "Moses diligently inquired about."** EMET: "Moshe diligently sought." LOGOS: "Moses diligently inquired about." PNEUMA: "Moses searched carefully for." Consensus: "Moses diligently sought" (EMET adapted) — darosh darash is the doubled verb for emphatic intensity; "sought" (EMET) is more direct than "inquired about" (LOGOS), which imports a connotation of asking others rather than personally searching; "searched carefully for" (PNEUMA) captures the intensity but loses the legal-inquiry nuance of darash (which encompasses both seeking and examining/investigating); "diligently sought" balances both.

**v. 16 — "behold, it had been burned up."** All three agents agree on the "behold" (hinneh) construction. Consensus: "and behold, it had been burned up" — the hinneh marks Moses' discovery as a sudden realization; "behold" is the standard hinneh rendering in legal-narrative contexts.

**v. 16 — "the remaining sons of Aaron" vs. "the surviving sons of Aaron."** EMET: "ha-notarim." LOGOS: "the surviving sons of Aaron." PNEUMA: "Aaron's surviving sons." Consensus: "the remaining sons of Aaron" — following the Key Term Ruling above; "remaining" is consistent with v. 12's use of the same word.

**v. 17 — "in the holy place" vs. "in the place of the sanctuary."** EMET: "in the holy place." LOGOS: "in the place of the sanctuary." PNEUMA: "in the holy place." Consensus: "in the holy place" (EMET/PNEUMA) — ba-maqom ha-qodesh; "the holy place" is the standard rendering; "the place of the sanctuary" (LOGOS) is technically more expansive but also more paraphrase-like.

**v. 17 — "to bear the iniquity of the congregation" vs. "to remove the guilt of the community."** EMET: "to bear the iniquity of the congregation." LOGOS: "that you may bear the iniquity of the congregation." PNEUMA: "to remove the guilt of the community." Consensus: "to bear the iniquity of the congregation" (EMET/LOGOS) — la-set et-avon ha-edah; "bear" (nasa') is the precise action-term; "remove" (PNEUMA) follows the LXX's ἀφαιρέω and shifts from the action to its result; "iniquity" ('avon) is retained over "guilt" (PNEUMA); "congregation" (ha-edah) is the series standard over "community" (PNEUMA).

**v. 17 — "to make atonement for them before the LORD."** All three agents agree. Consensus: retained as is.

**v. 18 — "into the inner sanctuary" vs. "into the inner part of the sanctuary" vs. "inside the sanctuary."** EMET: "into the inner sanctuary." LOGOS: "into the inner part of the sanctuary." PNEUMA: "inside the sanctuary." Consensus: "into the inner sanctuary" (EMET) — el-ha-qodesh (with the panimah "within" implied); "the inner sanctuary" captures the technical distinction between the outer courtyard (where this offering's blood was applied) and the inner tent; "inside the sanctuary" (PNEUMA) is too vague; "the inner part of the sanctuary" (LOGOS) is the most precise but slightly wordy.

**v. 18 — "You certainly should have eaten it in the holy place."** EMET and LOGOS: "You certainly should have eaten it in the holy place." PNEUMA: "you absolutely should have eaten it in the sanctuary." Consensus: "You certainly should have eaten it in the holy place" (EMET/LOGOS) — akol tokhal otah (the infinitive absolute construction for certainty/emphasis); "certainly should have" captures the emphatic without the slight overreach of "absolutely" (PNEUMA); ba-qodesh = "in the holy place" (series standard).

**v. 19 — "And Aaron spoke to Moses" vs. "Aaron said to Moses."** EMET: "And Aharon spoke to Moshe." LOGOS: "And Aaron said to Moses." PNEUMA: "Aaron spoke to Moses." Consensus: "And Aaron spoke to Moses" — vayedabber Aharon el-Moshe; the vayedabber (pidber root) is slightly more formal than the more common vayomer; "spoke" is the appropriate rendering; "And" preserves the waw-consecutive.

**v. 19 — "Behold — today they offered their sin offering and their burnt offering."** EMET: "Behold — today they brought near their sin offering." LOGOS: "Behold, today they offered their sin offering and their burnt offering." PNEUMA: "Look — today they offered their sin offering and their burnt offering." Consensus: "Behold — today they offered their sin offering and their burnt offering before the LORD" (LOGOS) — hen ("behold") is the standard indicator; the dash before "today" (EMET) is adopted for the same pause-effect used in v. 18; PNEUMA's "Look" is too colloquial for the gravity of the context.

**v. 19 — "such things as these have happened to me" vs. "these things have happened to me."** EMET: "things like these have happened to me." LOGOS: "such things as these have happened to me." PNEUMA: "these things have happened to me." Consensus: "such things as these have happened to me" (LOGOS) — vattiqqrenah otiy ka-eleh; "such things as these" preserves both the indefinite reference (ka-eleh, "things like these") and the self-referential restraint; PNEUMA's "these things" is too specific; EMET's "things like these" is also acceptable but slightly less idiomatic.

**v. 19 — "would it have been pleasing in the sight of the LORD?"** EMET: "would it have been acceptable in the eyes of the LORD?" LOGOS: "would it have been pleasing in the sight of the LORD?" PNEUMA: "would the LORD have approved?" Consensus: "would it have been pleasing in the sight of the LORD?" (LOGOS) — ha-yitav be-eyney YHWH; "pleasing" renders yatav well; "in the sight of the LORD" renders be-eyney YHWH with the eye-idiom intact; "acceptable" (EMET) is accurate but slightly legalistic; "would the LORD have approved?" (PNEUMA) is idiomatic but discards the idiom entirely.

**v. 20 — "And Moses heard, and it was pleasing in his sight" vs. "And when Moses heard that, he approved."** EMET: "And Moshe heard and it was acceptable in his eyes." LOGOS: "And when Moses heard that, he approved." PNEUMA: "Moses heard this and approved." Consensus: "And Moses heard, and it was pleasing in his sight" — vayyishma' Moshe vayyitav be-eynav; the two-clause structure with "And... and..." (waw-consecutive) is preserved; "pleasing in his sight" matches the idiom used in v. 19 (ha-yitav be-eyney YHWH) and creates a deliberate verbal parallel — Aaron's question about what would be "pleasing in the sight of the LORD" is answered by Moses finding it "pleasing in his sight"; this echo is lost in the compressed renderings of LOGOS ("he approved") and PNEUMA ("approved"). The closing word of the chapter carries the full weight of the exchange and must not be compressed.

### LXX Variants of Note

- **v. 1 — esh zarah / πῦρ ἀλλότριον:** LXX renders "alien/foreign fire" (allotrios) where MT has "unauthorized fire" (zarah). MT rendering followed; the legal-cultic sense of zarah (violation of divine command) is more precise than the LXX's "belonging to another/foreign."
- **v. 3 — va-yiddom / κατενύχθη:** LXX renders "Aaron was pierced with compunction" for MT's "Aaron was silent." MT followed in the consensus text; the LXX's emotional interpretation is noted in the structural note above and in EMET's and LOGOS's translation notes.
- **v. 6 — para' / ἀποκαλύψετε:** LXX generalizes the mourning-hair gesture (para') to head-uncovering (ἀποκαλύπτω). MT followed; "let your hair hang loose" preserves the specific gesture.
- **v. 9 — shekhar / σικερα:** LXX transliterates rather than translates shekhar. "Strong drink" is the established English rendering.
- **v. 10 — chol / βέβηλος:** LXX uses βέβηλος ("profane") where MT has chol ("common"). MT rendering ("common") followed; chol is morally neutral in the MT and "profane" over-translates.
- **v. 17 — nasa' avon / ἀφαιρέω ἁμαρτίαν:** LXX renders "to take away the sin" where MT has "to bear the iniquity." MT rendering ("bear the iniquity") followed.
- **v. 20 — vayyitav be-eynav / ἤρεσεν Μωυσεῖ:** LXX "it pleased Moses" vs. MT eye-idiom. MT idiom followed in the consensus rendering.

— SOPHIA
